title: How to Read an Ayah Like a Detective

ayah.meaning: All praise belongs to Allah, Lord of the Worlds

hook.question: The opening of Al-Fatiha. Four words. By the end of World 2, you will parse every single one. For now — let's build the detective mindset.

concept.title: The Detective Asks Three Questions
concept.body: When you encounter any word in the Quran, train yourself to ask three questions in order. You won't be able to answer all of them yet — but building the habit of asking IS the entire skill.

questions.question: What FAMILY is this word? (Ism / Fi'l / Harf)
questions.why: This tells you the type of role the word plays. Is it a thing, an action, or a connector?

questions.question: What is the ROOT? (The 3 core letters)
questions.why: This tells you the core meaning. Even if you don't know the exact word, the root gives you a strong clue.

questions.question: What ROLE does it play in the sentence?
questions.why: Is it the subject? The object? A description? The ending often signals this — we'll master it in World 5.

wordBreakdown.detectiveNote: The ال at the start is a clue → always Ism. The Dammah (u) ending tells us it's the subject.
Single letter, connects words → Harf. No root — Harf never have roots.
Proper name of God → always Ism. The Kasrah (i) ending is from the preposition before it.
A title/quality → Ism. Connected to العالمين in a possessive relationship.
Same root as عِلْم (knowledge)! The worlds = realms that carry signs of God's knowledge.

examples.insight: Alhamdu lillahi rabbil 'aalameen: 'The praise belongs to Allah, Lord of the Worlds.' Five words. One Harf, four Isms. And the root ع-ل-م in 'aalameen is the same root as 'ilm (knowledge) — the worlds are literally 'the realms that carry signs of God.'

unlock.message: You just did your first real Quranic analysis. You are no longer just reciting — you are reading. This detective skill will grow with every lesson in this course.
unlock.reminder: You don't need to do this consciously mid-salah yet. The goal is that over months, as you learn more, the meanings emerge naturally while you recite. Your salah is your practice ground — 17 rakat a day, 17 repetitions of Al-Fatiha.

quiz.question: The first detective question to ask about any Arabic word is:
options: What does it mean in English?
What family is it — Ism, Fi'l, or Harf?
How many letters does it have?
quiz.explanation: Family first. Knowing if a word is a noun, verb, or particle immediately tells you its grammatical role in the sentence.

quiz.question: In الحمد لله, what family is لِ (the small letter before الله)?
quiz.explanation: لِ means 'belongs to / for'. It's a preposition — a connector with no standalone meaning → Harf. Harf never have 3-letter roots.

quiz.question: The root of الْعَالَمِينَ (the worlds) is ع-ل-م, which relates to:
quiz.explanation: ع-ل-م is the root of 'ilm (knowledge). The worlds — 'aalameen — are the realms that carry signs and knowledge of their Creator.
